review: C3 ship-readiness — model-download integrity verification + mobile CI
blockingReason: Two confirmed criticals defeat the C3 control on desktop (pre-existing-file bypass) and iOS (background-path SHA-256 downgrade). Must be fixed before merge.
hashSpotCheck: Per-model SHA-256 values spot-checked against HuggingFace LFS pointers where the sandbox network allowed; catalog/manifest internally consistent across TS/Swift/Kotlin/JSON for all 11 current entries.
topActions: C3-1 (CRITICAL) src/services/llm.service.ts:667-675 — verify pre-existing model file before trusting it; on failure delete + re-download.

C3-2 (CRITICAL) apple/QuenderinKit/Sources/QuenderinKit/BackgroundModelDownloader.swift:40-41 — resolve catalog id from download URL, not filename, so SHA-256 verification runs on the background path.
C3-3 (HIGH) android/quenderin-core/src/main/kotlin/ai/quenderin/core/ModelIntegrity.kt:26-27 — add streaming sha256Hex(File) and make it canonical; deprecate the in-memory ByteArray overload.
C3-4 (MEDIUM) android/.../JvmDownloadIO.kt:115-125 — delete partial dest on copyTo failure before rethrow; engine catch also truncates finalPath.
C3-5 (MEDIUM) scripts/export_catalog.py:69 — make sha256 optional (s_opt) to match Swift/Kotlin optional semantics.
C3-6 (MEDIUM) scripts/check_catalog_parity.py:57-63 — per-entry parse so the sha256 regex cannot cross entry boundaries.
C3-7 (LOW) apple/.../ModelDownloader.swift:51 — defer { try? handle.close() } to avoid fd leak on error/cancellation.
C3-8 (LOW) android/.../ModelDownloadEngine.kt:117-142 — store.remove(model.id) after integrity failure to clear stale FAILED state.

confirmedFindings.title: Pre-existing file bypasses integrity verification entirely (desktop early-return)
confirmedFindings.detail: The early-return block at lines 667-675 checks fs.existsSync(dest) and stats.size > 100_000_000 then returns immediately, emitting 100% progress. verifyModelIntegrity is never called on a pre-existing file. This defeats C3: (a) a file planted at the model path before first launch is handed to the GGUF parser without GGUF-magic or SHA-256 verification, and (b) a previously-clean file later corrupted on disk is silently accepted on every subsequent app launch. The threat model explicitly names silently-truncated transfers and llama.cpp RCE CVEs. verifyModelIntegrity IS called at line 818 but only inside the download path, which the early-return bypasses entirely. Reported independently by both the ts-desktop and consistency dimensions.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: Inside the size check call await verifyModelIntegrity(dest, entry.sha256); on success emit 100% and return; on failure unlink dest + metaPath and fall through to re-download.

confirmedFindings.title: BackgroundModelDownloader protocol path silently skips SHA-256 — modelId is filename, not catalog id
confirmedFindings.detail: The ModelDownloader protocol conformance calls download(from:to:modelId: destination.lastPathComponent). lastPathComponent is the filename (e.g. qwen3-14b.Q4_K_M.gguf), not the catalog id (qwen3-14b). In didFinishDownloadingTo (line 124), resolved equals the filename; at line 135 ModelCatalog.entry(id: resolved) searches by id and always returns nil, so expectedSHA256 is always nil and ModelIntegrity.verify falls back to GGUF magic-header only. The full SHA-256 gate is silently bypassed for every download on the protocol path (OnboardingModel wiring). The foreground URLSessionModelDownloader.download(from:to:) (ModelDownloader.swift:83) resolves correctly via ModelCatalog.models.first { $0.downloadURL == url }. All catalog entries pin a SHA-256, so the omission is consequential for every production background download.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: let modelId = ModelCatalog.models.first { $0.downloadURL == url }?.id ?? destination.lastPathComponent; then call download(from:to:modelId:). Add a BackgroundModelDownloaderTests case driving a catalog-registered URL through the protocol path.

confirmedFindings.title: Android ModelIntegrity.sha256Hex takes ByteArray (in-memory) — OOM on multi-GB models; diverges from TS and iOS streaming twins
confirmedFindings.detail: Desktop sha256File (modelIntegrity.ts) and iOS sha256Hex(of:URL) both stream the file in chunks. The Android sha256Hex(bytes: ByteArray) signature requires the entire file in a ByteArray — a multi-GB GGUF causes OutOfMemoryError. Production sidesteps this via sink.sha256(tempPath) (ModelDownloadEngine.kt:123), so it does not fire today, but the public canonical utility is mistyped for its stated purpose and any future caller or test helper that hashes a real file path will OOM. NOTE: the kotlin-android dimension's own copy of this finding was rated low and marked isReal=false (speculative there); the consistency dimension confirmed it HIGH under the cross-platform-equivalence lens, which governs.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: Add fun sha256Hex(file: java.io.File): String streaming in 1MB chunks; make it canonical (ModelDownloadEngine calls it); mark/deprecate the ByteArray overload internal for small-buffer tests.

confirmedFindings.title: finalize() cross-filesystem fallback leaves corrupt final file on partial copy failure
confirmedFindings.detail: finalize() deletes dest (line 119), tries atomic renameTo (line 120), and on cross-filesystem failure falls back to copyTo(dest, overwrite=true) then temp.delete() (lines 122-123). If copyTo throws mid-write, the exception propagates to ModelDownloadEngine's outer catch (line 139) which sets FAILED and rethrows but does NOT delete finalPath. Result: finalPath partially written/corrupt, tempPath intact. An app-layer File(finalPath).exists() check finds a corrupt file and hands it to llama.cpp without re-running the C3 gate. The same-filesystem renameTo path is atomic and unaffected.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: In finalize(): try { temp.copyTo(dest, overwrite=true); temp.delete() } catch (e) { runCatching { dest.delete() }; throw e }. Also have the engine catch block call sink.truncate(finalPath) alongside marking FAILED.

confirmedFindings.title: export_catalog.py treats sha256 as required but the type system declares it optional
confirmedFindings.detail: s('sha256') at line 69 calls sys.exit() if sha256 is absent from a TS entry. But sha256 is Optional in Swift (String? at ModelCatalog.swift:46) and Kotlin (String? = null at ModelCatalog.kt:43), and magic-only verification is valid when sha256 is nil/null. The script's own docstring documents adding a model to constants.ts before refresh_model_hashes.py runs; in that documented intermediate state export_catalog.py crashes FATAL and blocks the manifest-regeneration pipeline instead of emitting null. Latent today (all 11 entries carry a hash).
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: Add an optional s_opt() helper returning None when the key is absent; use 'sha256': s_opt('sha256').

confirmedFindings.title: check_catalog_parity.py parse_named regex crosses model entry boundaries when sha256 is absent
confirmedFindings.detail: parse_named uses one re.DOTALL span with .*? between fields, ending at the sha256 literal. A sha256-less entry makes the non-greedy .*? spill past the entry boundary and grab the next model's hash, pairing the wrong id with the wrong hash; an entry lacking sha256 at the end of the list is dropped (count mismatch -> wrong 'missing' diagnosis). Compounding: load_canonical() (line 98) uses m.get('sha256', '') so the manifest carries '' while parse_named borrows a neighbor's 64-char hash — a guaranteed false mismatch or false pass. Same trigger as C3-5 (new model before refresh). Latent today.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: Split catalog text into per-entry blocks first (split on ModelEntry / object-literal boundaries), then extract each field per-block without DOTALL, using an optional capture group for sha256 (absence -> None, never borrowed).

confirmedFindings.title: iOS URLSessionModelDownloader leaks FileHandle on download error
confirmedFindings.detail: FileHandle(forWritingTo: partial) opened at line 51 is closed explicitly only at line 74 on the happy path. If the for-try-await byte loop (line 58) throws (network error, Task cancellation -> catch at lines 95-98), the handle is never closed. No defer. Repeated failed downloads accumulate leaked fds until EMFILE.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: let handle = try FileHandle(forWritingTo: partial); defer { try? handle.close() }

confirmedFindings.title: Android DownloadStore retains FAILED state entry after integrity-check failure
confirmedFindings.detail: On magic/SHA-256 failure (lines 117-129) the engine calls sink.truncate(tempPath) and throws. The catch (lines 139-141) sets setState(FAILED) but does NOT call store.remove(model.id) (remove only happens on the success path at line 137). The stale FAILED record (bytesDownloaded reflects the now-truncated partial) persists until the next download() overwrites it via upsert(RUNNING). Any UI observer or WorkManager retry logic reading store state in the failure->retry window sees inconsistent resume accounting. iOS and desktop have no persistent store — platform-specific asymmetry.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: Add store.remove(model.id) before the rethrow (or document that the next download() atomically overwrites it).

confirmedFindings.title: FakeFileSink.sha256 returns hash of empty bytes for missing path; JvmFileSink.sha256 would throw FileNotFoundException
confirmedFindings.detail: FakeFileSink.sha256(path) returns ModelIntegrity.sha256Hex(files[path] ?: ByteArray(0)) — for a missing path it silently returns the SHA-256 of empty bytes (e3b0c44298fc1c149...) instead of throwing. Production JvmFileSink.sha256 opens a FileInputStream and throws FileNotFoundException for a missing file. No current test exercises this divergence (the engine only hashes after a successful write), so nothing is broken today, but it would mask a future regression where sha256 is invoked before the file is fully written. NOTE: this is a third confirmed low; the report groups the two highest-priority lows (C3-7, C3-8) in the LOW section and folds this alongside, but it is a distinct finding. Severity-count lows = 2 (C3-7, C3-8) per the headline; this seam item is the additional confirmed low recorded here for completeness.
confirmedFindings.suggestedFix: override fun sha256(path: String): String = files[path]?.let { ModelIntegrity.sha256Hex(it) } ?: throw java.io.FileNotFoundException("FakeFileSink: no file at $path").

rejectedFindings.title: No test coverage for empty-file case in verifyModelIntegrity
rejectedFindings.reasoning: readHead returns buf.subarray(0, bytesRead) (bytesRead=0 for empty), hasGGUFMagic guards head.length >= 4 and correctly returns false, so verifyModelIntegrity already throws ModelIntegrityError on an empty file. The 'rejects a non-GGUF file' test (line 57-58) already exercises the same hasGGUFMagic=false branch. Missing coverage of an already-correct path is a test-quality observation, not a code defect.

rejectedFindings.title: ModelIntegrity.sha256Hex ByteArray API-safety-trap (kotlin-android dimension copy)
rejectedFindings.reasoning: Within the kotlin-android dimension this was rejected as speculative — no current caller misuses it (engine uses sink.sha256). The SAME underlying issue was independently confirmed HIGH by the consistency dimension as a cross-platform divergence (recorded as C3-3). The HIGH framing governs; this row documents the dimension-local rejection only.

rawDimensionResults.title: Desktop C3 (TypeScript)
confirmed: Pre-existing file bypasses integrity verification entirely
rejected: No test coverage for empty-file case in verifyModelIntegrity

rawDimensionResults.title: iOS C3 (Swift)
confirmed: BackgroundModelDownloader protocol path silently skips SHA-256 — modelId is filename, not catalog id

rawDimensionResults.title: Android C3 (Kotlin)
confirmed: finalize() cross-filesystem fallback leaves corrupt final file on partial copy failure
FakeFileSink.sha256 returns hash of empty bytes for missing path; JvmFileSink.sha256 would throw FileNotFoundException
rejected: ModelIntegrity.sha256Hex takes a ByteArray — a future caller doing file re-verification would OOM (rejected as speculative in this dimension; re-confirmed HIGH by consistency dimension)

rawDimensionResults.title: Catalog data + sync tooling
confirmed: export_catalog.py treats sha256 as required but the type system declares it optional
check_catalog_parity.py parse_named regex crosses model entry boundaries when sha256 is absent

rawDimensionResults.title: Cross-platform equivalence + regressions
confirmed: Desktop pre-existing model file bypasses all integrity checks (== C3-1)
Android ModelIntegrity.sha256Hex takes ByteArray — OOM; diverges from TS/iOS streaming twins (C3-3, HIGH)
iOS URLSessionModelDownloader leaks FileHandle on download error (C3-7)
Android DownloadStore retains FAILED state entry after integrity-check failure (C3-8)

residualRisk: Catalog tooling (C3-5, C3-6) detonates on the documented 'add model before refresh_model_hashes.py' step — highest residual risk after the criticals.
OOM trap (C3-3) is routed around in production but the public API still invites a multi-GB in-memory hash.
Cross-filesystem Android path (C3-4) only fires for SD-card users; won't show in CI.
fd-leak (C3-7) and stale-FAILED (C3-8) are reliability/UX papercuts; no integrity impact.
All findings confidence=medium: grounded in cited code, not dynamically reproduced (no end-to-end model download in this review).
